features.name: Spot & Preemptible VMs
features.description: Highly discounted (up to 91%) compute instances that Google can reclaim with a 30-second warning; Spot has no max duration while Preemptible is capped at 24 hours.

features.name: Live Migration
features.description: GCP technology that automatically migrates running VM instances to another host in the same zone during host maintenance, ensuring zero downtime.

features.name: Sole-Tenant Nodes
features.description: Dedicated physical compute servers running only your VMs, crucial for security, compliance, and custom hardware/licensing requirements.

features.name: Committed Use Discounts (CUDs)
features.description: Deep discounts (resource-based or flexible vCPU/RAM commitments) in exchange for signing a 1-year or 3-year contract.

features.name: Persistent Disk Snapshots
features.description: Zonal or regional block storage snapshots stored in Cloud Storage, supporting incremental backups and scheduling.

iamRoles.description: Full control over all Compute Engine resources, network, and security.
Permission to create, modify, and delete virtual machine instances, disks, and images.
Permissions to configure networking, firewalls, and routing, but not VM creation.

cliExamples.description: Create a custom-sized VM with startup script and cloud-platform scope
SSH into an instance through Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) without public IP

design: Compute Engine virtual machines run on KVM virtualization and support custom vCPU and memory configurations.
Zonal persistent disks are replicated across multiple physical disks in a single zone to prevent data loss.
Regional persistent disks replicate data synchronously across two zones in the same region, providing an RPO of zero.
Local SSDs are physically attached to the server hosting the VM, offering extremely high IOPS and low latency.
Data on Local SSDs is ephemeral and is lost when the VM instance is stopped, deleted, or undergoes host failure.
Sole-tenant nodes allow you to run instances on dedicated physical hardware for compliance and software licensing.
Custom metadata can be queried by the VM from the metadata server at http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/.
Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) support autoscaling, autohealing, and multi-zone deployment of identical VM templates.
security: Access scopes set the default authorization scopes for API requests made from the VM's service account.
Best practice is to assign the 'cloud-platform' scope to the VM and restrict permissions using IAM roles on the service account.
OS Login allows managing SSH access to VM instances using IAM credentials, replacing metadata-based SSH keys.
Service Account User role (roles/iam.serviceAccountUser) is required to attach a service account to a VM.
Shielded VMs protect instances from boot-level malware and rootkits by validating boot state using UEFI and vTPM.
Firewall rules are applied at the network level but evaluated at the individual VM instance level using network tags.
Compute instances without external IP addresses can access Google APIs using Private Google Access in the subnet.
Disk encryption defaults to Google-managed keys, but can be configured with CMEK or Customer-Supplied Keys (CSEK).
reliability: Live Migration is enabled by default, allowing VMs to move automatically to other hardware during infrastructure updates.
VM shutdown scripts are guaranteed to run on a best-effort basis for up to 90 seconds (longer on request).
Regional MIGs distribute VM instances across multiple zones in a region, protecting against single-zone outages.
Autohealing uses HTTP(S) health checks to automatically recreate unhealthy VM instances in a Managed Instance Group.
Backup VM configurations using custom images or machine images, which capture disk state, metadata, and configuration.
Spot VMs have an SLA of 0% and are subject to termination at any time, requiring stateless, fault-tolerant workloads.
Always design instances to be stateless; configure startup scripts to pull configuration from Cloud Storage on boot.
Zonal outages do not affect regional persistent disks, allowing prompt recovery by attaching disks to a VM in another zone.
cost: Resource-based Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) apply to vCPUs and memory for predictable workloads over a 1 or 3-year term.
Flexible CUDs provide spending-based discounts across VM families and regions, offering greater architectural flexibility.
Spot VMs offer a 60% to 91% discount compared to on-demand pricing, ideal for batch jobs and testing.
Custom machine types allow matching resources exactly to application needs, avoiding cost overhead from over-provisioning.
Persistent disks are billed based on provisioned capacity, not used capacity, so size them carefully.
Delete unattached persistent disks and unused static external IP addresses to avoid idle resource charges.
Enable autoscaling in MIGs to scale down instance counts during low-demand periods and save compute costs.
Configure VM scheduling (using Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Functions) to shut down dev/test VMs overnight.

features.name: GKE Autopilot
features.description: A fully managed cluster configuration mode where Google provisions, configures, scales, and manages nodes, security, and operations.

features.name: Workload Identity
features.description: The recommended way for applications running in GKE to securely access Google Cloud services by mapping Kubernetes service accounts to IAM service accounts.

features.name: Binary Authorization
features.description: A deploy-time security control that ensures only trusted container images signed by authorized keys are deployed to GKE.

features.name: Cluster Autoscaler
features.description: Automatically resizes the GKE node pool based on pod resource demands, scaling down to zero when resources are idle.

iamRoles.description: Full access to GKE clusters, node pools, Kubernetes APIs, and Kubernetes configuration.
Allows managing Kubernetes deployments, pods, services, and workloads, but not modifying the cluster itself.
Read-only access to cluster configurations and nodes.

cliExamples.description: Create a regional GKE Autopilot cluster with private nodes and authorized networks
Get cluster credentials and configure kubectl

design: GKE Autopilot mode shifts node management, provisioning, scaling, and security patching to Google Cloud.
GKE Standard mode provides full control over the underlying VM nodes, custom machine types, and node pools.
Regional clusters distribute master replicas and nodes across three zones in a region, maximizing control plane SLA.
Zonal clusters run the master control plane and nodes in a single zone, exposing them to zonal failures.
Workload Identity eliminates the need to export and manage JSON service account keys in Kubernetes Secrets.
GKE integrates with Cloud Load Balancing, automatically configuring external Ingress via Google Front End (GFE).
Node pools allow grouping VMs with identical machine types and labels to schedule workloads on specific hardware.
Pod address ranges must be planned carefully during subnet design to avoid running out of IPs in private clusters.
security: Private GKE clusters hide node external IPs; nodes communicate with the internet via Cloud NAT.
Master Authorized Networks restricts access to the Kubernetes control plane API to specified CIDR blocks.
Kubernetes Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) manages fine-grained permissions inside the namespace, while IAM secures cluster control.
Binary Authorization enforces policies that prevent containers from running if they lack signatures from trusted build pipelines.
Shielded GKE nodes protect the host against boot-level rootkits by validating kernel signatures.
Enable Network Policies using Calico to restrict pod-to-pod communication inside the cluster (east-west traffic).
Encrypt Kubernetes Secrets at rest using keys managed by Cloud KMS (envelope encryption).
Container Threat Detection in Security Command Center monitors container runtime activity for suspicious behaviors.
reliability: Enable Node Auto-Repair so GKE automatically recreates nodes that fail health checks.
Enable Node Auto-Upgrade to keep nodes up-to-date with security patches and Kubernetes releases automatically.
Deploy workloads across multiple zones using Pod Anti-Affinity rules to ensure high availability.
Use Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to scale pod replicas based on CPU utilization or custom metrics.
Configure Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) to adjust CPU and memory requests/limits for pods automatically.
Maintain GKE cluster configuration and manifests in a Git repository to enable GitOps-based disaster recovery.
GKE Autopilot control plane SLA is 99.95% for regional clusters and 99.5% for zonal clusters.
Set pod resource requests and limits correctly so Kubernetes scheduler can distribute pods reliably across nodes.
cost: GKE Autopilot bills you based on the CPU, memory, and storage requested by your running pods, not node capacity.
GKE Standard bills for the underlying Compute Engine VMs, persistent disks, and load balancers running in your project.
A management fee of $0.10 per hour per cluster is charged, but GCP offers one free zonal cluster per billing account.
Use Spot VM node pools in GKE Standard for fault-tolerant, stateless batch workloads to save compute costs.
Configure Cluster Autoscaler to scale node pools down to zero when no pods are scheduled on them.
Utilize the GKE cost optimization dashboard in Cloud Console to identify overallocated CPU/RAM requests.
Choose Autopilot over Standard for low-overhead operational costs, reducing engineering hours spent on cluster management.
Establish resource quotas at the Kubernetes namespace level to prevent single teams from over-consuming cluster budget.

features.name: Serverless Container Execution
features.description: Runs stateless HTTP containers on a fully managed Knative-based infrastructure, scaling up instantly and down to zero.

features.name: Concurrency Tuning
features.description: Allows configuring the maximum number of concurrent requests sent to a single container instance, reducing cold starts.

features.name: Serverless VPC Access
features.description: Enables Cloud Run services to securely connect to resources inside a VPC (like Cloud SQL, Memorystore) using private IPs.

features.name: Traffic Splitting
features.description: Supports routing percentages of incoming traffic across multiple revisions for canary deployments or A/B testing.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, update, delete, and manage Cloud Run services, traffic, and configurations.
Permissions to send requests and trigger public or private Cloud Run services.
Read-only access to service definitions, revisions, and configurations.

cliExamples.description: Deploy a container image to Cloud Run with internal-only ingress and VPC connector
Grant public unauthenticated access to a Cloud Run service

design: Cloud Run executes containerized code serverlessly, supporting any programming language, binary, or library.
Services scale dynamically from zero to thousands of instances based on incoming request traffic.
A single container instance can handle up to 250 concurrent requests, reducing cold starts compared to FaaS.
Revisions are immutable snapshots of code and configuration; deploying changes creates a new revision.
Integrates directly with Eventarc to trigger container invocations based on events from 90+ GCP services.
Cloud Run jobs run batch workloads that perform finite execution tasks to completion instead of web endpoints.
Connect to databases using Cloud SQL Auth Proxy built-in integration or Serverless VPC Access connector.
Supports custom domains with automated SSL/TLS certificate provisioning and renewal.
security: By default, Cloud Run services require authenticated requests; grant public access using roles/run.invoker for allUsers.
Use IAM service accounts to give Cloud Run containers specific permissions to other GCP resources (e.g. Secret Manager).
Integrate Cloud Run with Cloud Armor by placing it behind an external HTTP(S) Load Balancer and limiting direct ingress.
Secret Manager variables can be mounted as volumes or environment variables, avoiding storing secrets in the container image.
Ingress control settings allow restricting access to internal traffic or internal plus load balancer traffic only.
Use VPC Service Controls perimeters to protect Cloud Run workloads from data exfiltration risks.
VPC egress settings let you route all outbound traffic from Cloud Run through your VPC network to apply firewall rules.
Enforce Binary Authorization policies on Cloud Run to restrict deployments to approved images.
reliability: Cloud Run automatically replicates containers across multiple zones within the deployed region for high availability.
Min instances setting keeps a specified number of containers active, eliminating cold starts for latency-sensitive apps.
Max instances setting acts as a safety limit to prevent run-away autoscaling and protect backend systems from overload.
HTTP health checks can be configured to verify instance readiness before routing traffic to them.
Configure automatic retries and custom timeouts for backend container HTTP requests (up to 60 minutes).
Traffic splitting allows safe canary rollouts, routing e.g. 5% of traffic to a new revision and monitoring error rates.
Integrates with Cloud Logging out of the box; standard stdout/stderr logs are collected automatically.
Utilize Cloud Monitoring metrics to trigger alerts on container errors, request latency, and CPU usage.
cost: Charged on a pay-as-you-go basis, billed to the nearest 100 milliseconds of actual CPU and memory utilization.
Idle resources cost zero when a service scales down to zero instances, which is the default behavior.
Choose CPU allocation mode: 'CPU allocated only during request processing' (default) vs 'CPU always allocated'.
Always-allocated CPU is more expensive but reduces latency by allowing background tasks and faster warm starts.
Tune container concurrency limits to maximize CPU utilization per container instance, minimizing cost.
Set max instances to prevent high billing spikes from unexpected traffic bursts or DDoS attacks.
Optimize container startup times (smaller images, fast boot) to reduce billing during cold-start scaling.
Free tier offers 180,000 vCPU-seconds and 360,000 GiB-seconds of memory free per month.

features.name: Storage Classes
features.description: Offers Standard (active), Nearline (accessed < once/month), Coldline (< once/quarter), and Archive (long-term data retention).

features.name: Lifecycle Management
features.description: Rules to automatically delete or transition objects to cheaper storage classes based on age, creation date, or version count.

features.name: Object Versioning
features.description: Protects against accidental deletion by keeping history of object edits, allowing recovery of older object versions.

features.name: Signed URLs
features.description: Provides temporary, secure access to private storage objects without requiring Google credentials.

iamRoles.description: Full control over buckets, ACLs, and objects in Cloud Storage.
Full control over objects (create, read, delete), but cannot modify bucket metadata.
Read-only access to objects and their metadata.

cliExamples.description: Upload a directory to a Coldline bucket with multithreaded parallel upload
Generate a signed URL for reading a file that expires in 15 minutes

design: Cloud Storage is a globally unified, highly durable, serverless object storage system.
Storage classes determine performance and cost: Standard, Nearline (30d min), Coldline (90d min), and Archive (365d min).
Object names are flat keys; folders are simulated prefix listings (e.g. gs://bucket/folder/file.txt).
Object versioning helps recover from accidental overwrites, keeping active and noncurrent object states.
Dual-region storage replicates data synchronously across two distinct regions, offering 99.999% SLA.
Bucket Lock enforces WORM (Write Once Read Many) policies, making objects immutable for regulatory compliance.
Signed URLs grant time-limited access to specific objects using the signatures of a service account.
Storage Transfer Service transfers petabytes of data from other clouds (S3), HTTP endpoints, or on-premises.
security: Access control can be Uniform (bucket-level IAM only) or Fine-grained (using ACLs on individual objects).
Uniform bucket-level access is highly recommended for security compliance and ease of auditing.
All data in Cloud Storage is encrypted at rest by default; encryption can be CMEK or Customer-Supplied (CSEK).
Public access prevention (PAP) can be enabled on the bucket to prevent accidental exposure of sensitive data.
Data in transit is secured using HTTPS/TLS; access control supports VPC Service Controls security perimeters.
Do not use service account keys directly on clients; generate signed URLs or use Workload Identity.
Object lifecycle actions can delete noncurrent versions to enforce data retention compliance policies.
Audit logs track data access (Object Read, Object Write) and admin operations inside the bucket.
reliability: Cloud Storage offers 99.999999999% (11 9s) annual durability for all storage classes.
Multi-regional and dual-regional configurations replicate data across geographically separated datacenters.
Automatic geo-replication protects data from catastrophic regional disasters and data center failures.
Dual-region buckets have a recovery time objective (RTO) of zero during regional outages.
Standard storage has a 99.9% availability SLA, while Nearline/Coldline is 99.0% and Archive is 99.0%.
Strong global consistency ensures that after a write, updates are immediately visible to subsequent read requests.
Resume aborted uploads automatically using resumable upload sessions for large files (files up to 5TB).
Validate data integrity during transfers automatically using MD5/CRC32C hash checksum checks.
cost: Billed based on storage volume (GB/month), network egress, and operations (Class A: writes, Class B: reads).
Coldline and Archive classes have lower storage fees but higher access costs and minimum storage durations.
Archive storage costs as low as $0.0012 per GB/month, ideal for data kept for compliance purposes.
Retrieving data from Nearline, Coldline, or Archive incurs a retrieval fee per GB accessed.
Lifecycle management rules should automatically downgrade Standard data to Coldline/Archive over time.
Enable lifecycle rules to clean up incomplete multipart uploads after a specified number of days.
Transferring data between Cloud Storage buckets in the same region is free, but cross-region transfer incurs egress fees.
Reduce operational costs by combining many small files into larger archives before uploading.
